title: Overview
body:
Machine learning helps find patterns in training data to make predictions about new data. To get actionable results, you need to know: 

  • What data will best predict the target? 
  • Do you need to supplement or enrich the data?  
  • Are there other questions that this data can help answer?

title: course description
body:
Companies collect and store massive amounts of data—financial transactions, text and images scraped from social media, readings from system sensors, and so on. However, the goal is to have the right data to meet the defined project objectives. 


Because data quality is vital to the success of any machine learning project, data preparation is important. It is an iterative process that extends throughout the project; significant time is invested in finding, cleaning, and preparing data. In this course, we'll look at that process.


Course Topics

  • Data selection
  • High-level process
  • Dataset structure
  • Training dataset size
  • Data cleaning
  • Tools for understanding data



   Format:  Self-paced


   Duration:  30 minutes


   Audience:  Anyone who wats to understand the basic concepts of data preparation prior to using data with DataRobot. You should understand the concept found in What is AI / ML?


This is not a hands-on course for building models in the DataRobot platform. 


Last updated August 19, 2022.

title: Overview
body:
The best datasets reflect deep insights into the nature of the predictive problem, the context of the data sources, and the actions that will be based on predictions. It is essential to have a thorough understanding of all data used in modeling. You should review all your datasets prior to use to determine if they are appropriate and free from bias, but you also need to really get comfortable with the data. Work with SMEs to gain deep understanding.


There are also tools and techniques that can support your work. Fortunately, you will find that you will find  many helpful tools within DataRobot.

title: Question 1

_items.text: A row for every trade
A row for every trader
A row for every stock symbol
A row for every trader / stock symbol combination

body: What would be a good choice of unit of analysis if the target is to predict whether a stock trader engages in insider trading?

title: Dataset Description
body: The target is the feature which represents the final outcome that you are trying to predict. In a regression problem, the target is numeric, in a classification problem the target is a category. The rest of the columns are data features that could potentially help predict the target.

title: Unit of Analysis
body:
The unit of analysis.determines the level of granularity that each row represents in your data. 


Why is this important?  Because the level of granularity in your model's predictions should match the level of granularity in your project objective's level of aggregation. Let's look at an example.

title: Intro
body: Each row contains data from a historical example supported by features in each column that describe it.

body: What information has been lost?

title: Issue
body:
With the consolidated table, there is no way to know exactly how the original table was constructed. Whenever you can’t reverse a process, you know that information has been lost.


In your projects you need to determine whether removing information is acceptable and if not, how to add it back into your dataset.

body: What do you look for with Learning Curves to determine that adding more rows of data would improve prediction performance?

title: Learning Curve
body: One way to explore the impact of dataset size on a project is to generate models based on different size samples and see how that influences model performance. Start with with small samples, assess their performance. Then increase the size.

_graphic.caption: A learning curve shows model performance improvements relative to increasing dataset size.

title: Overview
body:
There’s a common perception that using all available data is necessary to build a good predictive model. In practice, using the largest possible training datasets can be unnecessary and even counterproductive.


Consider the relative size of these datasets. Despite the huge variation, many training datasets are closer to the range of a few hundred to a few thousand rows.

title: Example
body: You want to predict the likelihood that an employee will resign within one year of hire. You have five years of data. So, you build models trained on a one-year, a three-year, and a five-year datasets to determine the optimal tradeoff between using more data and using older data.

title: Number of Features
body:
Along with the number of rows, you should consider how many features are optimal. More is usually better, if they have a relationship to the target. 


In the following example, there is no reasonable correlation between the two features, space launches and sociology degrees. That is why should work closely with a subject matter expert to ensure that there is a plausible connection between each feature and the target.

_graphic.caption: The number of worldwide non-commercial space launches tracks closely with the number of sociology doctorates awarded in the US (Vigen, n.d.)

title: Question 2

_items.text: It doesn’t matter. Dataset size is not an important factor in model performance.
You should compare models with different size data samples to determine what works best.
You should eliminate all older data because it will degrade the model.
Use the largest size possible for the best results.

body: How much data should you use to build a model?

title: Question 3

_items.text: It will not have an impact.
It will improve model performance.
It will prevent model creation..
It will modestly degrade model performance.

body: What is the likely effect of adding a feature column that contains random numbers to your dataset?

title: Summary
body:
Remember, this isn't the end. Even if you’re successful in generating a dataset that is representative of new incoming data, all machine learning models eventually degrade and need to be replaced. Remember that models are trained on a static dataset that represents the state of the world at a given point in time.


Scheduling Retraining or Monitoring Drift

You can retrain new models on a predefined schedule, or decide whether retraining is necessary by monitoring drift in the distribution of a model’s features.

Whenever you decide to retrain, you need to have actual values for the target being predicted to do supervised learning. 


Put this at the end and in the other course

Data validation confirm that its high quality, consistent, and properly formatted for downstream processes. 

  • Do you have enough data for your needs?
  • Is it uniformly formatted in a design or language that your analysis tools can work with?
  • Does your clean data immediately prove or disprove your theory before analysis?

  • Validate that your data is regularly structured and sufficiently clean for your needs. Cross check corresponding data points and make sure nothing is missing or inaccurate.

title: Overview
body:
Data cleaning ensures that your modeling data is correct and consistent to improve the accuracy of your final results. You generally do this by removing corrupt, incorrect, or irrelevant data from your dataset(s). 


In this section, we will look at this process conceptually, but DataRobot has automates a lot of this work.

_items.title: Remove Irrelevant Data
_items.body: Look at your data and determine what is relevant and what unnecessary. Remove any data (rows or features) that do not contribute to the problem that you are trying to solve.

_items.title: Remove Duplicate Data (deduplicate)
_items.body: When you collect data from multiple sources, surveys organizations, or scrape data you often end up with duplicates. These duplicates may unbalance your model and skew your results.

Look for blank spaces in text, unanswered survey responses, blank cells, empty rows or columns, etc.This could be due to incomplete data, or data entry error. 


DataRobot automatically handles missing values as follows.

  • General linear algorithms - replaces missing numeric values with the median value and missing categorical values with the most common value. 

  • Tree-based algorithms - replaces missing values with an extreme value.


A general approach you should take prior to importing data:

  • If the target feature is missing a value, exclude that row.
  • If another feature is missing a value, investigate why the values are sometimes missing. Is there an operational process causing the inconsistency? 
  • If you know how the missing values should be replaced, consider replacing them yourself rather than having DataRobot automatically replaced them by averages or most common values.
_items.title: Deal with Missing Data
Determine the Impact of Outliers
_items.body: Outlier data falls far outside of the norm. They have the potential to skew your results. Consider the type of problem that you are trying to solve and the impact that the outlier will have on your results. For example, outliers are important to anomaly detection.

_items.title: Ensure Formatting Consistency
_items.body:
This includes removing misspellings and ensuring consistent use of terminology. It also means ensuring consistent formatting (dates, phone numbers, currency, etc.).


Inconsistencies are often the result of merging datasets from multiple sources or changes in system rules over time.

_items.title: Remove PII (If Appropriate)
_items.body: Removing personally identifiable information (PII) is important when you need to anonymize data to protect confidentiality.PII includes  email addresses, names, physical addresses, company names, phone numbers.

title: Example

_items.body: Do you see any issues with this dataset?
Notice there are two rows for Thomas Brown. Let's remove the duplicate.
Next, focus on data entry issues. Notice the inconsistencies (California, Calif., CA) and the missing value. Also, does it make sense that one row has an annual salary of 5260?

We standardized with two-letter state abbreviations. Since there was no way to determine the actual value for the missing one, we replaced it with “NULL.” as it is recognized  by systems as missing, 


After some research, we discovered a missing 0 for the salary.

instruction: Click on the arrows to walk through the data cleaning process.
body: You are preparing a dataset for a machine learning project that predicts employee retention. Here is a sample.

title: Filtering
body:
Detailed inspection of your data will help you detect problems with data quality and gain insights into how to enrich the dataset. 


Note: DataRobot has many tools to assist with this process, this section provides a high-level overview of some of them, you should take the AutoML Foundations I & II learning paths to learn them in detail.


Recurring words (keyword clouds)

title: Documentation
body:
A best practice is to document the information about your datasets. Just as in a database you would use a data dictionary to describe the contents, format, structure, and relationships between the elements of a database. For a dataset, you could comment this info in the AI catalog:

  • The meaning of each feature and its values.

  • A description of the operational processes that generate the feature.

  • Cautions or concerns about the quality of the data.

James: Is this an accurate description?

title: Graphic Title
body:
Here is a sample of the information that you see when you import a dataset into DataRobot.

  • For each feature
    • The number of unique and missing values
    • Mean, maximum, and minimum values (numeric features)
    • Most common and least common values (categorical features)
  • Importance relative to their relation to the target.
  • The data quality assessment to determine potential sources of issues such as outliers, missing values, or potential target leakage.

title: Text Title
body:
Sometimes features are interpreted as the incorrect data type. For example, a number might actually represent a category, such as a department code in an employee dataset. The codes have been randomly assigned and have no quantitative value. 


employee
department
salary
Rivera, Juan
5252600
Li, Su
1745467
Ang, Andi
5560384
Brown, Lequan
2267221
McGinn, Susan
55356738


One technique is to add a letter before each number so that it will be interpreted as a category rather than a number. 


employee
department
salary
Rivera, Juan
D5252600
Li, Su
D1745467
Ang, Andi
D5560384
Brown, Lequan
D2267221
McGinn, Susan
D55356738


DataRobot supports multiple methods of feature engineering—automatic and manual feature transformations for single datasets, as well as Feature Discovery for multiple datasets.
displayTitle: Ensure that data types are interpreted correctly

title: Overview
body:
Naive use of historical data can result in predictive models that perpetuate or even amplify unfair bias. To use predictive models responsibly, you need to mitigate the potential for bias by carefully reviewing:

  • Your sources of data
  • How the data is prepared
  • The impact of using the data


DataRobot University has courses that cover these issues in detail. However, there are some basic data preparation tasks that you can perform to address historical bias in your training data.

_items.body: Some protected or sensitive group attributes to consider are gender, race, ethnicity, color, language, religion, disability, age, sexual orientation, pregnancy, political opinion, medical record, criminal record, marital or relationship status, trade union activity, and genetic information. You should only include these attributes in training data for good, explicit, legal reasons.
_items.title: Remove protected classes from features

_items.body: To ensure that groups in the training data have equal success rates, you can rebalance the training dataset. This would mean removing rows selectively so that the positive rates for two groups become the same. explicit, legal reasons.
_items.title: Rebalance the training dataset

_items.body: To conform to the desired measure of group fairness, you can recalibrate your output predictions to conform to the desired measure of group fairness. To do this, you would adjust your prediction threshold.
_items.title: Recalibrate output predictions

_items.body: Another issue is that the home loans dataset example is not geographically diverse. All of the ZIP codes shown are for urban areas of the eastern United States. The model might be less accurate, and perhaps unfair, if used in the rural west. In general, models are best used to make predictions for cases that are similar to the data used to create them.
_items.title: Ensure diversity

Replace the missing value yourself based on subject matter knowledge.
Replace all missing values with "NULL."
Make sure the missing values are recognizable by the automated modeling system you are using.

title: Process Overview
body:
The machine learning lifecycle is a common framework to show the iterative stages from use case ideation through implementation. 


In this course, we focus on the acquire and explore data aspect of the process.While it is listed as one phase, in reality it's not that simplistic. You will likely iterate and revisit stages as appropriate, gathering more information and fine tuning.
displayTitle: Data Acquisition and the Machine Learning Lifecycle

title: Characteristics

_items.body: The range of data that the rows cover. Good datasets are highly diverse to account for a wide range of variability of scenarios.
_items.title: Diversity

_items.body: Enables you to consistently generate useful predictions. Data inconsistency can originate from data entry errors, noise introduced by fluctuating sensor readings, omitted or duplicate values (data uploaded multiple times), etc.
_items.title: Reliability

_items.body:
Data directly relates to the goal. For example, it is appropriate from a historical perspective and does not introduce bias that can skew results. 


There are many unintentional areas of bias, such as surveys (self-selection) or manufacturing models that use sensor data from a single piece of equipment rather than samples from multiple.


Bias can skew results by using overly training data does not appropriately represent the geography, age, race, sex, or income of the population that you are trying to model.
_items.title: Representative

body:
You need to ensure that training data is high quality. But, what does that mean?  


A quality dataset enables you to accomplish the outcome of the stated problem reliably and without introducing unnecessary bias. Here are some considerations:

title: Where it Comes From
body:
There are potential sources: databases, spreadsheets, text files, image files, media files, sensors, transactions, clickstream files, system logs, Internet of Things devices, and so on. 


These sources could be:

  • Internal (from your organization's systems) - generally contains more targeted information.
  • External 3rd party - from outside services such as, marketing survey data or credit reporting agencies.
  • ,Public - from government agencies or other open sources including census data, economic indicator data, weather information, or social media.


Sometimes data is stored in centralized repositories or data lakes. However, often it resides in disparate locations with some files stored locally, on-prem, while other are others in the cloud, and still more data in specialized devices.

title: Process Categories

_items.backText: Determine where the sources of data exist and if they are complete (you have all the data you need for an effective predictive model).

Clean, transform, and enrich your datasets. While you'll need to do some work,  you'll let the tools do a lot of the work for you. You'll have to format your data into a single table for use in DataRobot. But, there are things that you will likely do later in the process, like feature engineering, sampling and splitting the data because DataRobot has tools to help you with this.

Determine the target, unit of analysis, amount of training data needed for a good predictive model, enrichment data needs, and potential issues related to bias. 


You will engage subject matter experts and leverage DataRobot to help you with your analysis.

body: There is no one correct way to think about this, but there are some general areas to address.

title: Monitor.
body: Your should not think of this as a static, one-time process. Over time, conditions will change. Once you have solid models in production, you will need to monitor them for drift and determine if you need to retrain your models with fresh data that is more representative of the current state.

title: Datatype
body: Each feature has a specific data type associated with it, such as numeric, text, or date. However, this is not as straightforward as is it may seem because it's important to understand how it will be interpreted in your model. Consider the following examples:

_items.title: Numerical
_items.body:
Quantitative data that uses exact numbers where it has meaning as a measurement--a price, a number of people, a range of temperatures, 


Numerical data can be continuous (a value within a range) or discrete (with distinct values).

_items.title: Categorical
_items.body:
Categorical data represents characteristics, and is often used in classification problems.  It can be simple boolean true/false. Or, it could be a type, such as class levels: beginner, intermediate, or advanced. 


You can use numbers to represent categories, such as 1=blue, 2=green, 3=yellow. In this context, the classification has no 

numerical value.

_items.title: Date / Time Series
_items.body: Stores a calendar data (and time where appropriate). A times series contains a sequence of numbers collected at regular intervals over a set period of time. This enables you to look for trends over time, like sale prices of homes. Time series data has an implied order.

_items.title: Text
_items.body: Stores strings of characters such as words, like your name, a product description, or user feedback in a survey.

title: Some Datatypes aren't  What They Appear to Be
displayTitle: Not all Data Types are What They Appear to Be
body: Some data appears to be one data type, but is treated that way by the computer.  For example, a product sku 888-3333 or phone number may appear to be numerical, but do not have a value associated with them.  Fortunately, tools like DataRobot can help you manage these transformations.

title: Project Size
body:
Example
Size
Netflix titles
17,000
Amazon packages shipped**
1,600,000
YouTube channels
37,000,000
eBay listings*
1,700,000,000
Google searches*
2,000,000,000,000

*annual 2021 **daily 2021

title: Feature Impact
body:
Use Feature Impact to determine how strongly a feature relates to the outcome of the model. This helps determine if there are features you can remove.


It may also help gain insights into new types of data you might want to add to your dataset.

title: Histogram
body: Use histograms to understand the frequency distribution of values of numeric features. It groups values into bins (x-axis). The y-axis counts the number of rows in each bin.

_graphic.caption: This is an example learning curve in DataRobot.

title: Feature effects
body:
Use Feature Effects to look at model performance in the context of varying feature values, perhaps by showing groups or segments in the data where the model performs poorly. 


This raises a common issue when building models—should you include information about different groups as a feature within a single dataset, or break out the groups into multiple datasets and model each group separately? You can sometimes improve accuracy with these segmented models.

title: Word Cloud
body: Use the Word Cloud to visually display the most relevant words and short phrases in a dataset.

title: Adding More Data
body:
If they’re available, you can also iterate on the dataset by joining additional sources of data. Adding new data sources and features can often contribute more signal for the algorithms to pick up on. Providing a more complete picture of the world is often the most effective way to improve results.  


What if you don’t have access to additional data sources? Generating new data sources with new data collection efforts is a good strategy if you can do it. Absent that, you can still investigate the models in your existing DataRobot projects more closely for improvement ideas.
